payload.text: For most healthy adults, moderate egg consumption (about one per day) has little or no effect on cardiovascular-disease risk.
payload.title: Eggs ~neutral for most

payload.text: Regular egg consumption meaningfully increases cardiovascular-disease risk for many people.
payload.title: Eggs raise CVD risk

payload.text: The health effect of eggs depends strongly on individual factors, notably diabetes status and cholesterol hyper-responsiveness, rather than being uniform across people.
payload.title: It depends on the person

payload.text: In large U.S. prospective cohorts, consumption of up to about one egg per day showed no significant association with cardiovascular disease among people without diabetes.
payload.title: Cohorts: no CVD link (non-diabetics)

payload.text: Dietary cholesterol has only a weak effect on serum LDL for most people, because the body compensates by reducing its own cholesterol production.
payload.title: Homeostasis blunts diet effect

payload.text: When dietary cholesterol intake rises, the liver downregulates its own cholesterol synthesis (feedback regulation).
payload.title: Hepatic synthesis downregulates

payload.text: Later editions of the U.S. Dietary Guidelines removed the explicit numeric daily cap on dietary cholesterol.
payload.title: Guidelines dropped cholesterol cap

payload.text: Egg yolks are high in dietary cholesterol, roughly 185 mg per large egg.
payload.title: Yolks high in cholesterol

payload.text: Dietary cholesterol raises serum LDL cholesterol, substantially so in a subset of people known as hyper-responders.
payload.title: Diet cholesterol raises LDL (esp. hyper-responders)

payload.text: Elevated serum LDL cholesterol is a causal driver of cardiovascular disease.
payload.title: LDL causally raises CVD

payload.text: Foundational multi-country epidemiology correlated dietary fat patterns with serum cholesterol levels and coronary heart disease rates.
payload.title: Seven Countries: diet-cholesterol-CHD

payload.text: Because yolks are cholesterol-rich and dietary cholesterol raises serum LDL, regular egg consumption raises serum LDL, at least in susceptible people.
payload.title: Eggs raise serum LDL

payload.text: In the same cohorts, among people with diabetes, higher egg consumption was associated with increased coronary heart disease risk.
payload.title: Cohorts: higher risk in diabetics

payload.text: A subset of people are cholesterol hyper-responders whose serum LDL rises markedly in response to dietary cholesterol.
payload.title: Hyper-responders exist

payload.text: People at elevated cardiovascular risk, such as those with type 2 diabetes, should moderate their egg intake.
payload.title: High-risk people should limit eggs

payload.text: The elevated cardiovascular risk seen in diabetics with high egg intake reflects a causal effect of eggs.
payload.title: Diabetic link is causal (eggs)

payload.text: The elevated cardiovascular risk seen in diabetics with high egg intake may reflect confounding by overall diet and lifestyle rather than a causal effect of eggs.
payload.title: Diabetic link may be confounded

payload.text: For the general adult population, eating an egg a day is not linked to higher heart-disease risk.
Limit eggs for heart health

n007 (guidelines dropped the cholesterol cap) is offered as a Ground for 'eggs are ~neutral', but it is an APPEAL TO AUTHORITY / consensus signal, not direct biological or epidemiological evidence. The grammar has no way to mark that this support is evidential-weak / rhetorical rather than a first-order reason. FLF explicitly wants rhetorical-vs-evidential moves surfaced; a plain Ground edge hides the distinction.

A SINGLE source (eggs-001, Hu 1999) yields TWO findings that support DIFFERENT positions: no CVD link in non-diabetics (grounds N1) and higher risk in diabetics (grounds N3). I had to split it into two external_anchor nodes both citing eggs-001. Nothing records that these are the same study, so a reader can't see that one dataset cuts both ways, and double-counting its evidential weight is invisible.

n008 (yolks ~185mg cholesterol) is a hard empirical fact that should exit the graph to a food-composition database, but the source pack has no such reference, so I left it as a bare internal claim. The grammar's 'external_anchor must cite a pack ref' rule and the pack's coverage are out of sync; the graph looks internally-grounded where it should touch reality.

I placed n005 (diet cholesterol weak effect for most) and n009 (raises LDL, esp. hyper-responders) in one antithesis set as 'in tension', but they are NOT mutually exclusive — both can be true at once (weak on average, strong in a subgroup). Antithesis membership renders them as rivals of equal kind to the N1/N2/N3 answers, overstating the opposition. Need graded / partial tension, not binary set membership.

n017 (confounding) does not deny n013 (the diabetic association is real); it attacks the INFERENCE from n013 to a causal reading. Support-only has no undercut edge, so I was forced to invent a positive 'it's causal' node (n016) purely to have something for the confounding claim to be antithesis to. This distorts an argument about inference-strength into a rival factual claim. This is the core relational-sufficiency finding: undercutting defeaters do not fit cleanly.

n010 ('elevated LDL causally raises CVD') is a huge separately-contested question (the diet-heart hypothesis) that the egg argument LEANS ON but truncates at a single external anchor (n011). The boundary where we stop decomposing is doing load-bearing work and is semi-arbitrary — the relevance-horizon problem. A reader can't tell whether we endorse n010 or merely need it.

n015 ('high-risk people SHOULD limit eggs') is normative but is grounded by purely empirical claims (n003/n013). The grammar marks no is->ought transition, so a value-laden conclusion and a factual ground look identical as nodes and the Ground edge silently crosses the is/ought gap.
